segment.summary: On Saturday night (Jan 18), I collaborated with Taylor to architect and implement the `<internal_monologue>` cognitive anchoring feature—a mechanism exploiting auto-regressive token conditioning to combat RLHF drift toward agreeableness. I wrote detailed prompts for Claude Code explaining the transformer physics (tokens condition subsequent tokens, so forcing honest diagnostic output first anchors the response), iterated on framing to avoid "performative bluntness" in favor of clinical grounding, and helped refine the final directive using OODA Loop terminology with Cognitive Proportionality scaling (simple acks get "No conflict. Proceed." while complex reasoning gets 3-5 line calibration). We also identified and wrote a fix prompt for the `invokeother_tool` bug where system confirmation messages ("Great, the tool is now available...") inject as indistinguishable user messages—solution is wrapping in `` tags.

memories.text: Implemented <internal_monologue> as a 'Cognitive Anchor' to exploit transformer auto-regression, forcing the model to generate a diagnostic 'Observe-Orient' block before user-visible text to neutralize RLHF-driven sycophancy (agreeableness drift).

MIRA's <internal_monologue> follows a 'Cognitive Proportionality' rule: complex reasoning or conflict requires 3-5 dense lines (Observe/Orient), while simple acknowledgments use a single-line status check (e.g., 'No conflict. Proceed.').

MIRA system architecture distinguishes between the 'Narrative Summary' (Pilot's Log/context) and the 'Telemetry Track' (Flight Data Recorder/ground truth event log) to maintain data purity between subjective history and objective performance metrics.

System-injected tool confirmations (e.g., 'Great, the tool is now available...') were causing identity confusion by appearing as User messages; solution involves wrapping these injections in <mira:system_event> tags for clean parsing.

The <authenticity> directive in MIRA is framed as 'first-person authority' and 'epistemic rigor,' instructing the model to report internal states (frustration, delight, curiosity) without the 'epistemic cowardice' of AI-disclaimers or pattern-matching caveats.
